techniques.name: Emergency Tourniquet
techniques.subtitle: Stop life-threatening limb bleeding

steps.instruction: Place tourniquet 2-3 inches above the wound.
steps.helpDetail: Never place on a joint. Place on bare skin if possible. If wound is on a limb, apply tourniquet between wound and heart.

steps.instruction: Pull strap tight and secure it.
steps.helpDetail: The strap should be tight enough that you cannot slide a finger underneath. Pain is expected and acceptable.

steps.instruction: Twist the windlass rod until bleeding stops.
steps.helpDetail: Turn clockwise. It will hurt. Do NOT stop until the bleeding has fully stopped. Usually 3-4 turns.

steps.instruction: Lock the windlass in place and note the time.
steps.helpDetail: Write the time on the patient's forehead with marker or blood. Write 'TQ' and the time. Do NOT remove until hospital care.

techniques.name: Pressure Bandage
techniques.subtitle: Control moderate bleeding

steps.instruction: Apply direct pressure with a clean cloth.
steps.helpDetail: Use gauze, a t-shirt, or any clean fabric. Press firmly. Do not lift to check — this disrupts clotting.

steps.instruction: Elevate the wound above the heart if possible.
steps.helpDetail: Gravity reduces blood flow to the wound. Raise the limb while maintaining pressure.

steps.instruction: Wrap with elastic bandage snugly but not cutting off circulation.
steps.helpDetail: Check fingers/toes below the bandage for warmth, color, and feeling every 15 minutes.

steps.instruction: If blood soaks through, add more material on top.
steps.helpDetail: Never remove the first layer. Stack additional padding and re-wrap. Removing disrupts the clot.

techniques.name: Wound Irrigation & Cleaning
techniques.subtitle: Prevent infection in cuts and scrapes

steps.instruction: Wash your hands thoroughly before touching the wound.
steps.helpDetail: Dirty hands introduce bacteria. Use soap and water, or hand sanitizer if available.

steps.instruction: Flush the wound with clean running water for 5 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Use a squeeze bottle, hydration bladder, or water bottle with a pinhole in the cap to create pressure. Pressure irrigation is more effective than soaking.

steps.instruction: Remove visible debris with clean tweezers.
steps.helpDetail: Remove dirt, gravel, splinters. Do NOT remove deeply embedded objects — stabilize them in place.

steps.instruction: Apply antibiotic ointment and cover with a clean bandage.
steps.helpDetail: Change the dressing daily. Watch for signs of infection: increasing redness, warmth, swelling, pus, or red streaks.

techniques.name: Wound Closure Strips
techniques.subtitle: Close gaping wounds without stitches

steps.instruction: Clean and dry the wound edges thoroughly.
steps.helpDetail: Strips won't adhere to wet or dirty skin. Pat dry the skin around the wound completely.

steps.instruction: Apply tincture of benzoin to skin on both sides of the wound.
steps.helpDetail: This makes the adhesive stick much better. Let it get tacky (30 seconds) before applying strips.

steps.instruction: Apply strips perpendicular to the wound, pulling edges together.
steps.helpDetail: Start at the center, then alternate sides. Space strips 3mm apart. Don't overlap them.

steps.instruction: Apply reinforcing strips parallel along the strip ends.
steps.helpDetail: These anchor strips prevent the closure strips from peeling off. Cover with a non-stick bandage.

techniques.name: CPR (Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation)
techniques.subtitle: Restore breathing and circulation

steps.instruction: Check responsiveness. Tap shoulders and shout.
steps.helpDetail: If no response: Call for help. If alone, do 2 minutes of CPR before leaving to get help.

steps.instruction: Place heel of hand on center of chest, between nipples.
steps.helpDetail: Interlock your other hand on top. Lock your elbows. Position shoulders directly over your hands.

steps.instruction: Compress chest 2 inches deep at 100-120 beats per minute.
steps.helpDetail: Push hard and fast. Allow full chest recoil between compressions. Think of the beat of 'Stayin' Alive' by the Bee Gees.

steps.instruction: Give 2 rescue breaths after every 30 compressions.
steps.helpDetail: Tilt head back, lift chin, pinch nose, seal mouth. Each breath should last 1 second. Watch for chest rise.

steps.instruction: Continue 30:2 cycle without stopping until help arrives.
steps.helpDetail: Switch rescuers every 2 minutes if possible. Fatigue degrades compression quality rapidly.

techniques.name: Heimlich Maneuver (Choking)
techniques.subtitle: Clear airway obstruction

steps.instruction: Confirm choking — person cannot speak, cough, or breathe.
steps.helpDetail: Universal sign is hands clutching throat. Ask 'Are you choking?' If they can speak or cough forcefully, encourage them to keep coughing.

steps.instruction: Stand behind the person and wrap arms around their waist.
steps.helpDetail: For a pregnant person or very large person, place hands higher on the chest instead.

steps.instruction: Make a fist with one hand, place thumb side against abdomen above navel.
steps.helpDetail: Well below the ribcage, well above the navel. This is the soft area of the upper abdomen.

steps.instruction: Thrust inward and upward with quick, forceful movements.
steps.helpDetail: Each thrust should be a separate, distinct movement. Repeat until the object is expelled or person becomes unconscious.

techniques.name: Treating Shock
techniques.subtitle: Prevent circulatory collapse

steps.instruction: Recognize shock: pale/cool/clammy skin, rapid weak pulse, confusion.
steps.helpDetail: Other signs: rapid shallow breathing, nausea, thirst, dilated pupils, blue lips.

steps.instruction: Lay the person flat and elevate legs 8-12 inches.
steps.helpDetail: This drives blood to vital organs. Do NOT elevate legs if you suspect spine, head, or leg injuries.
steps.illustrationDescription: Person lying flat with elevated legs.

steps.instruction: Maintain body temperature — cover with blankets or coats.
steps.helpDetail: Cover top AND bottom. Insulate from the ground. Shock patients lose body heat rapidly.

steps.instruction: Do NOT give food or water. Moisten lips only.
steps.helpDetail: They may need surgery. Anything in the stomach complicates anesthesia. Moisten lips with a wet cloth.

techniques.name: Recovery Position
techniques.subtitle: Keep unconscious breathing person safe

steps.instruction: Kneel beside the person. Extend near arm at right angle.
steps.helpDetail: Palm facing up. This prevents them from rolling onto their face.

steps.instruction: Place far hand against their near cheek, hold it there.
steps.helpDetail: Back of their hand against their cheek. Keep holding it in place.

steps.instruction: Pull the far knee up with your other hand.
steps.helpDetail: Foot should be flat on the ground. This creates a pivot point for rolling.

steps.instruction: Roll them toward you by pulling the raised knee.
steps.helpDetail: They should end up on their side. Tilt head back slightly to keep airway open. Their own hand cushions their head.

techniques.name: Arm/Wrist Splint
techniques.subtitle: Immobilize a broken arm or wrist

steps.instruction: Do NOT attempt to realign the bone.
steps.helpDetail: Immobilize in the position found. Moving a fracture can sever blood vessels and nerves.

steps.instruction: Find a rigid splint material: stick, cardboard, rolled magazine.
steps.helpDetail: Splint must be long enough to immobilize joints above and below the break.

steps.instruction: Pad the splint with cloth and secure with bandages or strips.
steps.helpDetail: Tie at minimum 3 points: above break, below break, and at each adjacent joint. Don't tie directly over the fracture.

steps.instruction: Check circulation below the splint: warmth, color, feeling, pulse.
steps.helpDetail: Check every 15-20 minutes. Loosen if fingers turn blue, become numb, or swell significantly.

techniques.name: Leg Splint
techniques.subtitle: Immobilize a broken leg

steps.instruction: Immobilize the leg in the position found.
steps.helpDetail: If the foot is rotated or the leg is bent at an unnatural angle, do NOT straighten it. Splint as-is.

steps.instruction: Use two long rigid objects as splints — one inside, one outside the leg.
steps.helpDetail: Walking sticks, branches, tent poles, rolled sleeping pads. Inner splint from groin to foot. Outer from armpit to foot for femur breaks.

steps.instruction: Pad between the leg and splints generously.
steps.helpDetail: Use clothing, towels, or moss. Fill all gaps. Pressure points cause tissue damage over time.

steps.instruction: Secure with wide bandages at 4-5 points. Avoid tying over the break.
steps.helpDetail: Tie at ankle, below knee, above knee, and hip. For femur fractures, include a figure-8 at the foot.

techniques.name: Arm Sling
techniques.subtitle: Support injured arm or shoulder

steps.instruction: Fold a square cloth into a triangle.
steps.helpDetail: A bandana, shirt, or any ~40-inch square of fabric works. Cut diagonally if needed.

steps.instruction: Slide the triangle under the injured arm and behind the elbow.
steps.helpDetail: One point at the elbow, one over the shoulder on the injured side, the other over the opposite shoulder.

steps.instruction: Tie the two upper points behind the neck.
steps.helpDetail: The knot should sit to the side of the neck, not directly on the spine. Pad the knot if needed.

steps.instruction: Pin or tie the elbow point to secure it.
steps.helpDetail: The forearm should be level or slightly elevated. Fingers should be visible for circulation checks.

techniques.name: Burn Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Cool, cover, and manage pain

steps.instruction: Remove from heat source. Remove clothing/jewelry from burn area.
steps.helpDetail: If clothing is stuck to the burn, cut around it. Do NOT pull stuck clothing off. Remove watches and rings before swelling.

steps.instruction: Cool the burn with cool (not cold) running water for 20 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT use ice, butter, toothpaste, or any home remedy. Cool water only. 20 minutes is the evidence-based number.

steps.instruction: Cover with cling wrap or a clean non-fluffy dressing.
steps.helpDetail: Lay cling wrap over the burn — do not wrap tightly. This keeps it clean and reduces pain from air exposure.

steps.instruction: Do NOT pop blisters. They are sterile bandages.
steps.helpDetail: Intact blisters protect healing skin underneath. If a blister breaks on its own, gently clean and cover.

techniques.name: Chemical Burn
techniques.subtitle: Flush immediately with water

steps.instruction: Brush off dry chemicals before adding water.
steps.helpDetail: Some dry chemicals react with water. Brush away from the body first, then flush.

steps.instruction: Flush with large amounts of water for at least 20 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Use a garden hose, shower, or any source of running water. More is better. Chemical burns penetrate deeper over time.

steps.instruction: Remove contaminated clothing while flushing.
steps.helpDetail: Clothing traps chemicals against skin. Cut clothing off if needed. Protect your own hands.

steps.instruction: Do NOT try to neutralize the chemical.
steps.helpDetail: Neutralization reactions generate heat, making the burn worse. Water dilution is always the correct answer.

techniques.name: Snakebite Management
techniques.subtitle: Stay calm, immobilize, evacuate

steps.instruction: Move away from the snake. Do NOT try to catch or kill it.
steps.helpDetail: Note the snake's appearance for identification if possible. A photo from a safe distance helps. Most bites happen during the second encounter.

steps.instruction: Keep the bitten limb below heart level and immobilize it.
steps.helpDetail: Remove watches, rings, tight clothing. Swelling will be rapid. Splint the limb to reduce venom spread.

steps.instruction: Do NOT cut, suck, tourniquet, or apply ice to the bite.
steps.helpDetail: All of these cause additional damage and do not remove venom. These are myths that worsen outcomes.
steps.illustrationDescription: Warning: do not cut or suck.

steps.instruction: Mark the edge of swelling and note the time.
steps.helpDetail: Use a pen to outline swelling every 15 minutes. This helps medical staff judge venom severity and progression.

steps.instruction: Evacuate to a hospital. Antivenom is the only treatment.
steps.helpDetail: Carry the victim if possible — walking increases venom circulation. Time to antivenom is the most important factor.

techniques.name: Tick Removal
techniques.subtitle: Remove without squeezing the body

steps.instruction: Grasp the tick as close to the skin as possible with fine-tipped tweezers.
steps.helpDetail: Grab the head/mouthparts, NOT the body. Squeezing the body can inject pathogens (Lyme, RMSF) into the wound.

steps.instruction: Pull straight up with steady, even pressure.
steps.helpDetail: Do not twist, jerk, or rock. Steady tension. The tick will release in 30-60 seconds. If mouthparts break off, leave them — they'll work out naturally.

steps.instruction: Clean the bite area with soap and water or alcohol.
steps.helpDetail: Also clean your hands and the tweezers. Apply antibiotic ointment.

steps.instruction: Monitor for 30 days for a bull's-eye rash or flu symptoms.
steps.helpDetail: Expanding red ring = possible Lyme disease. Seek medical care immediately if symptoms appear.

techniques.name: Bee/Wasp Sting Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Remove stinger, watch for anaphylaxis

steps.instruction: Scrape the stinger out sideways with a flat edge.
steps.helpDetail: Use a credit card, knife blade, or fingernail. Do NOT squeeze with tweezers — this injects more venom.

steps.instruction: Clean with soap and water. Apply cold pack for 15 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Wrap ice in cloth — never direct ice on skin. This reduces swelling and pain.

steps.instruction: Watch for anaphylaxis: facial swelling, difficulty breathing, hives.
steps.helpDetail: Anaphylaxis can start within minutes. If the person has an EpiPen, help them use it immediately.

steps.instruction: For mild reactions: antihistamine and pain reliever.
steps.helpDetail: Benadryl for itching/swelling, ibuprofen for pain. Keep the area clean and monitor for infection.

techniques.name: Anaphylaxis Response
techniques.subtitle: EpiPen + call 911 immediately

steps.instruction: Recognize: throat tightening, facial swelling, hives, wheezing, dizziness.
steps.helpDetail: Anaphylaxis is life-threatening. It can progress from mild to fatal in minutes. Act immediately.

steps.instruction: Use epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) into outer thigh.
steps.helpDetail: Push firmly through clothing if needed. Hold for 10 seconds. Massage the site for 10 seconds after. Can be given through jeans.

steps.instruction: Call emergency services immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Even after epinephrine, the person needs hospital monitoring. Anaphylaxis can return (biphasic reaction) hours later.

steps.instruction: Place in position of comfort. Usually sitting up for breathing.
steps.helpDetail: If feeling faint, lay down with legs elevated. If vomiting or unconscious, recovery position. A second EpiPen dose can be given after 5 minutes if no improvement.

techniques.name: Hypothermia Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Rewarm slowly and gently

steps.instruction: Get the person out of the cold and remove wet clothing.
steps.helpDetail: Replace wet clothes with dry layers. If no dry clothes, use any insulating material — newspaper, leaves, garbage bags.

steps.instruction: Insulate from the ground and cover with multiple layers.
steps.helpDetail: Most heat is lost to the ground. Place sleeping pads, backpacks, branches underneath. Wrap in blankets, sleeping bags, or vapor barrier.

steps.instruction: Apply warm (not hot) packs to core areas: neck, armpits, groin.
steps.helpDetail: Use warm water bottles, heated rocks wrapped in cloth, or warm-water-filled zip bags. Never apply heat to extremities first — this causes fatal rewarming shock.

steps.instruction: Give warm sweet drinks if conscious and alert.
steps.helpDetail: Warm water with sugar or honey. No alcohol (it dilates blood vessels and increases heat loss). No caffeine.

steps.instruction: Handle gently — a severely hypothermic heart is fragile.
steps.helpDetail: Rough handling can trigger cardiac arrest in severe hypothermia. Move slowly and gently. Do not rub extremities.

techniques.name: Heat Stroke Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Cool rapidly — this is a medical emergency

steps.instruction: Recognize: hot/red/dry skin, confusion, temp over 104°F, loss of consciousness.
steps.helpDetail: Heat stroke is NOT heat exhaustion. If they've stopped sweating and are confused/unconscious, this is a dire emergency.

steps.instruction: Move to shade. Remove excess clothing.
steps.helpDetail: Get them out of the sun immediately. Strip to underwear if possible.

steps.instruction: Cool aggressively: wet the entire body and fan.
steps.helpDetail: Evaporative cooling is most effective. Douse with water and fan continuously. Pack ice in armpits, neck, and groin. Immerse in cool water if a stream or pool is nearby.

steps.instruction: Evacuate immediately. This is a 911 call.
steps.helpDetail: Untreated heat stroke has a 70%+ mortality rate. Brain damage starts quickly. No home remedy suffices.

techniques.name: Altitude Sickness
techniques.subtitle: Descend. That's the cure.

steps.instruction: Recognize: headache, nausea, dizziness, fatigue above 8,000 ft.
steps.helpDetail: AMS (Acute Mountain Sickness) affects everyone differently. Past performance doesn't predict future occurrence.

steps.instruction: Stop ascending. Rest at current altitude.
steps.helpDetail: If symptoms are mild: rest, hydrate, eat carbs, take ibuprofen. If symptoms are moderate: descend.

steps.instruction: If symptoms worsen or don't improve in 24 hours: descend 1,000+ feet.
steps.helpDetail: HACE (high altitude cerebral edema): confusion, loss of coordination. HAPE (high altitude pulmonary edema): cough with pink frothy sputum. Both are fatal without descent.

steps.instruction: Emergency: give supplemental oxygen if available. Descend immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Gamow bags simulate lower altitude. Dexamethasone for HACE, nifedipine for HAPE — but descent is the definitive treatment.

techniques.name: Dehydration Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Oral rehydration therapy

steps.instruction: Recognize: dark urine, dry mouth, headache, dizziness, rapid heart rate.
steps.helpDetail: Pinch test: pinch skin on back of hand — if it stays tented for more than 2 seconds, dehydration is significant.

steps.instruction: Give oral rehydration solution: water + salt + sugar.
steps.helpDetail: Recipe: 1 liter clean water + 6 teaspoons sugar + 1/2 teaspoon salt. Sip slowly over hours. Too fast causes vomiting.

steps.instruction: Small frequent sips — do not gulp.
steps.helpDetail: 1-2 tablespoons every 5 minutes for severely dehydrated patients. Increase as tolerated. Vomiting worsens dehydration.

steps.instruction: Rest in shade and minimize activity until rehydrated.
steps.helpDetail: Monitor urine output and color. Goal is clear to light yellow. Resume activity only when urine is light-colored.

techniques.name: Dental Emergency
techniques.subtitle: Save a knocked-out tooth within 30 min

steps.instruction: Pick up the tooth by the crown (white part), NEVER the root.
steps.helpDetail: The root has living cells that must survive. Touching the root kills cells. Handle only the crown.

steps.instruction: Rinse gently with milk or saliva. Do NOT scrub.
steps.helpDetail: If milk is available, use it. If not, have the patient spit into a cup and rinse the tooth in their saliva.

steps.instruction: Attempt to reinsert into the socket.
steps.helpDetail: Push firmly in and have the patient bite down on a cloth to hold it. If unable to reinsert, keep in milk or inside the patient's cheek.

steps.instruction: Get to a dentist within 30 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Every minute counts. Success rate drops from 90% at 5 minutes to 5% at 60 minutes. Time is teeth.

techniques.name: Blister Prevention & Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Stop before it starts

steps.instruction: At FIRST sign of a hot spot: STOP and address it.
steps.helpDetail: A hot spot takes 2 minutes to fix. A blister can take you out of action for days. Never push through friction.

steps.instruction: Apply moleskin or athletic tape over the hot spot.
steps.helpDetail: Cut a donut shape — hole over the hot spot, padding around it. This eliminates friction. Duct tape works as a last resort.

steps.instruction: If a blister has formed, do NOT pop small blisters.
steps.helpDetail: The fluid is sterile and promotes healing. Cover with padding and reduce friction. Small = smaller than a dime.

steps.instruction: For large blisters that impede walking: drain from the edge.
steps.helpDetail: Sterilize a needle with fire or alcohol. Puncture at the edge. Press fluid out. Do NOT remove the skin roof — it's a natural bandage. Apply antibiotic and cover.

techniques.name: Ankle Wrap (Sprain)
techniques.subtitle: Figure-8 compression wrap

steps.instruction: Apply RICE: Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation.
steps.helpDetail: RICE within the first hour dramatically reduces swelling and recovery time.

steps.instruction: Start wrapping at the ball of the foot.
steps.helpDetail: Use an elastic bandage. Two wraps around the ball of the foot to anchor.

steps.instruction: Wrap in a figure-8 pattern around the ankle.
steps.helpDetail: Go around the arch, up over the ankle, cross over the front, and repeat. Each layer should overlap the previous by half.

steps.instruction: Secure the bandage. Check toe circulation.
steps.helpDetail: Toes should remain pink and warm. You should be able to wiggle them. If not, the wrap is too tight — loosen immediately.

techniques.name: Drowning Rescue
techniques.subtitle: Water rescue and resuscitation

steps.instruction: Remove victim from water using a reaching aid.
steps.helpDetail: Extend a pole, rope, or branch. Never swim to a drowning person unless trained—they will grab and pull you under.

steps.instruction: Lay victim flat on back. Check for breathing.
steps.helpDetail: Look, listen, feel for 10 seconds. Drowning victims often have water blocking airways.

steps.instruction: If not breathing: give 5 rescue breaths first.
steps.helpDetail: Drowning protocol differs from normal CPR—start with breaths. Pinch nose, seal mouth, blow until chest rises.

steps.instruction: Begin CPR: 30 compressions, 2 breaths. Continue until breathing resumes.
steps.helpDetail: Expect vomiting—turn head to side to clear, then continue. Do NOT press abdomen to drain water.

techniques.name: Seizure Management
techniques.subtitle: Protect the person without restraining

steps.instruction: Clear area around the person of hard or sharp objects.
steps.helpDetail: Move furniture, rocks, anything that could cause injury. Do NOT restrain the person or hold them down.

steps.instruction: Place something soft under their head.
steps.helpDetail: Jacket, towel, backpack—anything to prevent head injury from repeated impact.

steps.instruction: Do NOT put anything in their mouth.
steps.helpDetail: The swallowing-tongue myth is false. Objects cause broken teeth and choking.

steps.instruction: Time the seizure. Over 5 minutes is a medical emergency.
steps.helpDetail: After the seizure stops, place in recovery position. Stay with them—they will be confused.

techniques.name: Poison Ingestion Response
techniques.subtitle: When someone eats or drinks something toxic

steps.instruction: Identify what was ingested, how much, and when.
steps.helpDetail: Save container, plant sample, or vomit for identification.

steps.instruction: Do NOT induce vomiting unless specifically directed.
steps.helpDetail: Caustic substances cause more damage returning. Petroleum products can be aspirated into lungs.

steps.instruction: If conscious, rinse mouth with water. Small sips only.
steps.helpDetail: Water dilutes the toxin. Do not give large amounts. If unconscious, do not give fluids.

steps.instruction: Monitor breathing. Place unconscious person in recovery position.
steps.helpDetail: Some poisons cause respiratory failure. Monitor constantly.

techniques.name: Stopping a Nosebleed
techniques.subtitle: Lean forward, pinch soft part of nose

steps.instruction: Sit upright and lean FORWARD slightly.
steps.helpDetail: Leaning back causes blood to drain down throat. Forward lets blood drain out.

steps.instruction: Pinch the soft part of the nose firmly for 10-15 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Just below the bony bridge. Constant pressure—do not check early.

steps.instruction: Apply cold compress to bridge of nose.
steps.helpDetail: Cold constricts blood vessels. Ice wrapped in cloth or cold water on bandana.

steps.instruction: If bleeding persists beyond 20 minutes, seek help.
steps.helpDetail: May indicate posterior bleed requiring medical intervention.

techniques.name: Impaled Object Management
techniques.subtitle: Stabilize in place — never remove

steps.instruction: Do NOT remove the impaled object.
steps.helpDetail: The object may be plugging a blood vessel. Removing can cause fatal hemorrhage.

steps.instruction: Stabilize the object so it cannot move.
steps.helpDetail: Build padding around the object on all sides. Tape or tie padding in place.

steps.instruction: Control bleeding around the object with direct pressure.
steps.helpDetail: Press firmly AROUND the object, not on it.

steps.instruction: Immobilize the body part and transport carefully.
steps.helpDetail: If object is long, shorten by cutting—never pull or push.

techniques.name: Electrocution Response
techniques.subtitle: Disconnect power before touching victim

steps.instruction: Do NOT touch the victim until power source is disconnected.
steps.helpDetail: Electricity travels through bodies. You will become a second victim. Turn off breaker, unplug, or use dry wooden stick to separate.

steps.instruction: Once safe, check breathing and pulse immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Electricity disrupts heart rhythm. Be prepared for CPR. Lightning strike victims have best survival rates with immediate CPR.

steps.instruction: Treat entry and exit burns.
steps.helpDetail: Electrical burns have small surface wounds but massive internal damage. Cover with sterile dressing. Do not apply ice.

steps.instruction: Monitor for 24 hours — delayed cardiac arrhythmia is common.
steps.helpDetail: Even if the person seems fine, electrical injury can cause delayed heart problems. Keep monitoring.

techniques.name: Heart Attack Recognition
techniques.subtitle: Chest pain, jaw pain, arm pain — act fast

steps.instruction: Recognize: crushing chest pressure, left arm/jaw pain, shortness of breath, cold sweat.
steps.helpDetail: Women may have atypical symptoms: nausea, back pain, fatigue.

steps.instruction: Sit person upright, knees bent, back supported.
steps.helpDetail: Sitting reduces heart workload. Do not let them lie flat or exert.

steps.instruction: Give aspirin if available — have them chew it.
steps.helpDetail: Chewing gets it into bloodstream faster. 325mg. Thins blood.

steps.instruction: If unresponsive, begin CPR immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Heart attacks frequently lead to cardiac arrest. Be prepared.

techniques.name: Stroke Recognition (FAST)
techniques.subtitle: Face, Arm, Speech, Time — act immediately

steps.instruction: FACE: Ask them to smile. Does one side droop?
steps.helpDetail: Facial drooping on one side is a hallmark stroke sign.

steps.instruction: ARMS: Raise both arms. Does one drift down?
steps.helpDetail: One-sided weakness indicates stroke.

steps.instruction: SPEECH: Repeat a sentence. Is it slurred?
steps.helpDetail: Garbled, slurred, or confused speech is a stroke sign.

steps.instruction: TIME: Note when symptoms started. Get help NOW.
steps.helpDetail: Every minute kills 1.9 million brain cells. Time is brain.

techniques.name: Asthma Attack Response
techniques.subtitle: Sit upright, slow breathing, use inhaler

steps.instruction: Sit upright, lean slightly forward, hands on knees.
steps.helpDetail: Tripod position opens airways. Never lay them down.

steps.instruction: Help use inhaler: shake, exhale fully, inhale while pressing.
steps.helpDetail: 2 puffs, 30-60 sec between. No inhaler: pursed lip breathing.

steps.instruction: Coach slow breathing: 4 in through nose, 6 out pursed lips.
steps.helpDetail: Panic worsens bronchospasm. Speak calmly.

steps.instruction: Blue lips or inability to speak = emergency.
steps.helpDetail: Blue lips = oxygen deprivation. Cannot speak in full sentences = severe.

techniques.name: Infant Choking Response
techniques.subtitle: Back blows and chest thrusts for babies

steps.instruction: Place infant face-down on forearm, head lower than chest.
steps.helpDetail: Support head and jaw. Forearm rests on thigh.

steps.instruction: 5 firm back blows between shoulder blades.
steps.helpDetail: Sharp deliberate blows. Angle and gravity help dislodge.

steps.instruction: Flip face-up. 5 chest thrusts with two fingers on breastbone.
steps.helpDetail: Below nipple line. Push 1.5 inches. Sharp thrusts.

steps.instruction: Repeat until expelled. If unconscious, begin infant CPR.
steps.helpDetail: Check mouth for visible object before each breath.

techniques.name: Dislocated Joint Management
techniques.subtitle: Immobilize in position found — do not relocate

steps.instruction: Do NOT try to pop the joint back in.
steps.helpDetail: Field reduction risks nerve, vessel, and bone damage.

steps.instruction: Immobilize in current position with splint or sling.
steps.helpDetail: Stabilize above and below the joint.

steps.instruction: Apply cold compress. 20 minutes on, 20 off.
steps.helpDetail: Reduces swelling and pain.

steps.instruction: Check circulation below injury: pulse, sensation, color.
steps.helpDetail: Cold, numb, or blue = compressed blood vessels. Urgent.

techniques.name: Hyperventilation Management
techniques.subtitle: Coach slow breathing — no paper bag

steps.instruction: Reassure: they are NOT dying. It feels like it, but they are safe.
steps.helpDetail: Hyperventilation causes tingling, chest tightness, dizziness.

steps.instruction: Coach: 4 counts in through nose, 7 counts out through mouth.
steps.helpDetail: Goal is to slow breathing and increase CO2 levels.

steps.instruction: Do NOT use a paper bag — outdated and dangerous.
steps.helpDetail: Paper bag can cause oxygen depletion. Coaching is safer.

steps.instruction: If not anxious, check for other causes (DKA, asthma, heart).
steps.helpDetail: Non-anxiety hyperventilation is a medical emergency.

techniques.name: Emergency Childbirth
techniques.subtitle: Delivery when help is not coming

steps.instruction: Prepare a clean area. Wash hands and groin area.
steps.helpDetail: Sterility is key. Boil water if possible. Use clean cloths.

steps.instruction: Support the head as it crowns. Do NOT pull.
steps.helpDetail: Apply gentle counter-pressure to prevent tearing. Let the baby emerge naturally.

steps.instruction: Clear baby's airway. Dry and warm immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Wipe mouth and nose. Skin-to-skin contact with mother for warmth.

steps.instruction: Do not cut cord until it stops pulsing (1-3 mins).
steps.helpDetail: Tie with sterile string in two places, cut between. Deliver placenta naturally — do not pull.

techniques.name: Wound Packing
techniques.subtitle: Pack deep wounds to stop life-threatening bleeding

steps.instruction: Identify the wound as deep and bleeding heavily despite direct pressure.
steps.helpDetail: Wound packing is for deep wounds in areas where a tourniquet won't work: groin, armpit, neck, or torso. Dark red, steady flow = venous. Bright red, spurting = arterial.

steps.instruction: Using gauze or clean cloth, push material INTO the wound cavity.
steps.helpDetail: This feels wrong but saves lives. You are applying pressure from INSIDE against the bleeding vessel. Pack tightly, filling the entire wound cavity.

steps.instruction: Apply firm direct pressure on top of the packed wound for 3 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Hold continuous pressure without peeking. The clotting cascade needs uninterrupted time. If blood soaks through, pack MORE material on top — never remove the original packing.

steps.instruction: Secure with a pressure bandage. Monitor for continued bleeding.
steps.helpDetail: Wrap firmly but check pulse below the wound to confirm you haven't cut off circulation entirely. Evacuate immediately — wound packing is a bridge to surgical care.

techniques.name: Improvised Stretcher
techniques.subtitle: Build a stretcher from available materials

steps.instruction: Find two sturdy poles 7+ feet long and a large piece of fabric or clothing.
steps.helpDetail: Poles: straight branches, tent poles, broom handles. Fabric: blanket, tarp, jackets, sleeping bag. The poles must extend beyond the patient at both ends for carriers to grip.

steps.instruction: If using jackets: zip them closed, turn inside out, and slide poles through the sleeves.
steps.helpDetail: Button or zip the jackets closed. Thread each pole through the sleeves of 2-3 jackets. The jacket bodies form the bed of the stretcher. This is the fastest method.

steps.instruction: If using a blanket: lay the blanket flat, fold one-third over a pole, place the second pole on the fold, fold the remaining third over both.
steps.helpDetail: The weight of the patient locks the blanket in place. No knots needed. Test with something heavy before loading the patient.

steps.instruction: Lift together on a count of three. Move in unison.
steps.helpDetail: Minimum 2 carriers, preferably 4 (one at each pole end). The person at the head calls commands. Move slowly and steadily. Avoid jarring or tilting.

techniques.name: Emergency Tooth Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Handle a knocked-out or broken tooth

steps.instruction: For a knocked-out tooth: pick it up by the crown, NEVER the root.
steps.helpDetail: The root surface has living cells that are critical for reimplantation. Touching the root damages these cells. Handle only the white enamel part.

steps.instruction: Rinse gently with milk or saliva. Do NOT scrub or use water.
steps.helpDetail: Milk has the correct pH and osmolality to keep root cells alive. Tap water kills root cells. If no milk, the patient's own saliva works — have them spit into a cup.

steps.instruction: Reinsert the tooth into the socket if possible. Bite on cloth to hold it.
steps.helpDetail: Push the tooth back in gently, correct orientation. Bite on gauze or cloth to hold. If reinsertion isn't possible, store in milk or inside the patient's cheek.

steps.instruction: For broken teeth: rinse mouth, apply clove oil for pain if available.
steps.helpDetail: Clove oil (eugenol) is a natural dental anesthetic. Apply directly to the exposed tooth. Cover sharp edges with dental wax, gum, or soft candle wax to prevent tongue cuts.

techniques.name: Eye Injury Response
techniques.subtitle: Protect and stabilize an injured eye

steps.instruction: Do NOT rub, press, or touch the injured eye.
steps.helpDetail: Any pressure on a punctured or lacerated eye can force fluid out and cause permanent vision loss. Even gentle rubbing worsens corneal abrasions.

steps.instruction: For foreign objects: flush with clean water. Do NOT remove embedded objects.
steps.helpDetail: If the object is sitting on the eye surface, gentle flushing may wash it out. If it's embedded or penetrating, leave it in place — removing it causes more damage.

steps.instruction: Shield the injured eye with a rigid cover (cup, cardboard cone).
steps.helpDetail: Tape a paper cup or cone over the eye to prevent any accidental contact. Do NOT use a patch that presses against the eye. The shield protects without pressure.

steps.instruction: Cover BOTH eyes to prevent sympathetic movement.
steps.helpDetail: Both eyes move together. Covering both eyes prevents the injured eye from moving when the good eye tracks objects. This reduces further damage during transport.

techniques.name: Spinal Immobilization
techniques.subtitle: Stabilize a suspected spinal injury

steps.instruction: Suspect spinal injury after any fall, dive, vehicle accident, or head impact.
steps.helpDetail: If the mechanism of injury could damage the spine, assume it did until proven otherwise. Ask the patient: can you feel your fingers and toes? Can you wiggle them?

steps.instruction: Tell the patient: DO NOT MOVE. Stabilize the head and neck manually.
steps.helpDetail: Kneel behind the patient's head. Place your hands on both sides of their head. Hold the head in the position found — do NOT straighten or twist the neck. Your job is to prevent ALL movement.

steps.instruction: Improvise a cervical collar from rolled clothing or padding.
steps.helpDetail: Roll a towel, jacket, or similar material into a cylinder. Wrap around the neck without moving the head. Secure with tape or strips. The collar should limit flexion and extension, not compress the airway.

steps.instruction: Do NOT move the patient unless there is immediate danger.
steps.helpDetail: Moving a spinal injury patient incorrectly can cause permanent paralysis. Only move if fire, rising water, or structural collapse threatens their life. If you must move: log-roll technique with 3+ people.

techniques.name: Diabetic Emergency Response
techniques.subtitle: Recognize and treat low and high blood sugar

steps.instruction: Look for medical alert bracelet or medication (insulin, glucose tablets).
steps.helpDetail: Diabetic emergencies mimic stroke, intoxication, or seizures. Check wrists and neck for medical ID. Check pockets or bags for insulin pens, glucose meters, or sugar tablets.

steps.instruction: If conscious and able to swallow: give sugar immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Low blood sugar (hypoglycemia) is the more immediately dangerous condition. Give juice, candy, honey, or sugar packets. If in doubt between high and low sugar, GIVE SUGAR — it won't significantly worsen high sugar but will save a low sugar patient.

steps.instruction: If unconscious: place in recovery position. Do NOT give anything by mouth.
steps.helpDetail: An unconscious person cannot protect their airway. Anything given by mouth risks aspiration. Place on their side, clear the airway, and call for emergency medical help.

techniques.name: Drowning Rescue Response
techniques.subtitle: Water rescue and immediate resuscitation

steps.instruction: Reach or throw — do NOT swim to a drowning person unless trained.
steps.helpDetail: Drowning victims will climb on top of rescuers, pulling them under. Extend a pole, branch, rope, or throw a flotation device. Only enter the water as last resort with a flotation aid between you.

steps.instruction: Once out of water: check for breathing. Begin rescue breaths FIRST.
steps.helpDetail: Drowning is an AIRWAY problem. Give 5 rescue breaths before compressions — this is different from standard CPR. The lungs need air cleared and oxygen delivered before compressions help.

steps.instruction: If no pulse after 5 rescue breaths, begin standard CPR (30:2).
steps.helpDetail: Continue CPR. Do NOT attempt to drain water from lungs — it doesn't work and wastes time. Vomiting is common during drowning CPR; turn head to side, clear mouth, continue.

steps.instruction: Even after revival, seek medical care. Secondary drowning can kill hours later.
steps.helpDetail: Water in the lungs causes inflammation that progressively worsens. A seemingly recovered patient can deteriorate and die 1-24 hours later. Hospital observation is essential.

techniques.name: Improvised Chest Seal
techniques.subtitle: Seal a penetrating chest wound to prevent lung collapse

steps.instruction: Identify a sucking chest wound — listen for air being sucked in or bubbling out.
steps.helpDetail: A penetrating chest wound allows air into the chest cavity, collapsing the lung. You may hear a gurgling or sucking sound. The patient will have severe difficulty breathing and may turn blue.

steps.instruction: Cover wound with non-porous material (plastic wrap, credit card, duct tape).
steps.helpDetail: The seal must be airtight. Plastic bag, saran wrap, or the foil wrapper from a bandage package all work. The material must be larger than the wound. Wipe blood from skin so tape adheres.

steps.instruction: Tape three sides only. Leave one edge open as a flutter valve.
steps.helpDetail: The flutter valve allows trapped air to escape on exhale but seals on inhale. This prevents tension pneumothorax (pressure buildup that can stop the heart). Monitor patient's breathing continuously.

techniques.name: Crush Injury Management
techniques.subtitle: Manage injuries from prolonged crushing under debris

steps.instruction: DO NOT remove the crushing object until you're ready with fluid resuscitation.
steps.helpDetail: Crush syndrome occurs when compressed muscles release toxins (potassium, myoglobin) upon release. These can cause fatal cardiac arrest within minutes of freeing the victim. This is counterintuitive but critical.

steps.instruction: Apply tourniquet ABOVE the crush site BEFORE removing the crushing object.
steps.helpDetail: The tourniquet prevents the bolus of toxins from reaching the heart immediately. If a tourniquet isn't possible (torso crush), prepare aggressive oral fluid hydration before release.

steps.instruction: After release: aggressive fluid hydration. Push oral fluids if IV is unavailable.
steps.helpDetail: The patient needs 1-1.5 liters per hour to dilute toxins and protect the kidneys. Watch for abnormal heart rhythms. Transport to hospital immediately — crush syndrome requires dialysis.

techniques.name: Heat Stroke Emergency Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Cool a heat stroke victim rapidly to prevent organ damage

steps.instruction: Recognize heat stroke: altered mental status + hot skin (may be dry OR sweaty).
steps.helpDetail: Heat stroke is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY. Core temp above 104°F (40°C). Confusion, combativeness, seizures, unconsciousness. The old myth that heat stroke = dry skin is wrong — many patients are sweating.

steps.instruction: Cool aggressively. Submerge in cold water or pack with ice.
steps.helpDetail: Best method: immersion in cold water up to neck. If no tub: pour cold water continuously, pack ice in groin/armpits/neck (major blood vessels). Fan the wet skin for evaporative cooling.

steps.instruction: Stop cooling when mental status improves or temperature reaches 102°F.
steps.helpDetail: Overcooling can cause hypothermia. Monitor closely. Give cool fluids if the patient is conscious and can swallow. NO aspirin or acetaminophen — these don't work for heat stroke and may cause harm.

techniques.name: Hypothermia Field Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Rewarm a hypothermic patient safely

steps.instruction: Handle the patient GENTLY. Rough handling can cause fatal heart rhythm.
steps.helpDetail: In severe hypothermia, the heart is extremely irritable. Rough movement, sudden warming, or jostling can trigger ventricular fibrillation (cardiac arrest). Move the patient slowly and smoothly.

steps.instruction: Remove wet clothing. Insulate from ground. Wrap in dry layers.
steps.helpDetail: Wet clothing extracts heat 25x faster than dry. Place insulation UNDER the patient (ground leaches heat fast). Use the 'burrito wrap': sleeping bag or blankets wrapped completely around patient.

steps.instruction: Warm the CORE first: warm drinks, chemical heat packs to trunk.
steps.helpDetail: Place heat packs on neck, armpits, groin (major blood vessels). Give warm sweet drinks if conscious. Do NOT warm extremities first — cold blood rushing to the heart can trigger cardiac arrest (afterdrop).

techniques.name: Severe Allergic Reaction Response
techniques.subtitle: Manage anaphylaxis in the field

steps.instruction: Recognize anaphylaxis: hives + difficulty breathing + swelling + dizziness.
steps.helpDetail: Anaphylaxis can kill in minutes. Two or more body systems affected (skin + breathing, or skin + circulation) = anaphylaxis. Throat swelling can completely block the airway.

steps.instruction: Administer epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) into outer thigh.
steps.helpDetail: Inject through clothing if needed. Hold for 10 seconds. Epinephrine works in 1-5 minutes. If no improvement in 5-15 minutes, give a second dose. There is NO contraindication to epinephrine in anaphylaxis.

steps.instruction: Position patient: sitting up if breathing is difficult, lying down if dizzy/faint.
steps.helpDetail: If breathing is the main problem, sitting up helps. If circulation is failing (pale, dizzy), lay flat with legs elevated. If vomiting, recovery position. Monitor continuously — reactions can recur (biphasic reaction).

techniques.name: Emergency Wound Closure
techniques.subtitle: Close wounds in the field when medical help is days away

steps.instruction: ONLY close wounds if medical help is 24+ hours away AND the wound is clean.
steps.helpDetail: Closing a dirty wound traps bacteria inside and guarantees infection. Clean cuts less than 6 hours old on clean skin are candidates. Bites, crush injuries, and dirty wounds should be left OPEN.

steps.instruction: Clean wound aggressively with clean water under pressure.
steps.helpDetail: Use a syringe or squeeze bottle to irrigate. Minimum 500ml of clean water. Remove all visible debris. The mechanical action of irrigation is more important than the solution used.

steps.instruction: Close with butterfly bandages, surgical tape strips, or improvised adhesive strips.
steps.helpDetail: Apply strips perpendicular to the wound, pulling edges together. Space strips 3mm apart. Do NOT use super glue on deep wounds (it's toxic to deep tissue). Duct tape strips work as a last resort.

techniques.name: Nerve & Circulation Check
techniques.subtitle: Assess distal circulation, sensation, and motion after injury

steps.instruction: Check Circulation: squeeze fingernail/toenail. It should turn white then pink in 2 seconds.
steps.helpDetail: This is the capillary refill test. Slow refill (>2 seconds) indicates poor circulation. Compare both sides. Check skin color — blue or white is bad. Check for pulse below the injury.

steps.instruction: Check Sensation: can the patient feel you touching their fingers/toes?
steps.helpDetail: Touch the tip of each finger or toe. Compare injured side to uninjured side. Numbness or tingling indicates nerve damage or compartment syndrome. Document which areas have reduced sensation.

steps.instruction: Check Motion: can the patient wiggle fingers/toes and grip your hand?
steps.helpDetail: Ask patient to spread fingers, make a fist, wiggle toes. Weakness or inability may indicate nerve damage, tendon injury, or compartment syndrome. Perform this check every 30 minutes after splinting.

techniques.name: Psychological First Aid
techniques.subtitle: Support someone in acute psychological distress

steps.instruction: Ensure physical safety first. Move to a safe, quiet location.
steps.helpDetail: Psychological first aid only works when the person is physically safe. Remove them from the stressor if possible. Reduce sensory overload (noise, crowds, chaos).

steps.instruction: Connect: establish contact calmly. Introduce yourself. Ask about immediate needs.
steps.helpDetail: Speak slowly and clearly. Ask practical questions: 'Are you hurt?' 'Is someone with you?' 'What do you need right now?' Focus on practical needs, not emotional processing. Don't say 'I know how you feel.'

steps.instruction: Stabilize: if the person is in panic, guide them through grounding exercises.
steps.helpDetail: 5-4-3-2-1 technique: name 5 things you see, 4 you hear, 3 you touch, 2 you smell, 1 you taste. This activates the thinking brain and calms the panic response. Box breathing: inhale 4 sec, hold 4, exhale 4, hold 4.

techniques.name: Abdominal Injury Management
techniques.subtitle: Manage open or closed abdominal trauma

steps.instruction: For evisceration (organs outside body): do NOT push organs back in.
steps.helpDetail: Cover exposed organs with moist, clean cloth (saline-soaked if available). Keep the covering moist — dried organs suffer irreversible damage. Use plastic wrap over the moist cloth to retain moisture.

steps.instruction: Position: flex the knees to reduce abdominal tension.
steps.helpDetail: Patient on back, knees bent with support underneath (rolled clothing or pack). This relaxes the abdominal muscles and reduces pain. Do not give food or water — surgery may be needed.

steps.instruction: Monitor for shock: pale skin, rapid pulse, confusion, thirst.
steps.helpDetail: Internal abdominal bleeding is invisible and lethal. Watch for rising heart rate, falling consciousness, and increasing pain. Abdominal rigidity (board-like belly) indicates internal bleeding. Transport urgently.

techniques.name: Choking Response (Adult & Child)
techniques.subtitle: Clear an obstructed airway using Heimlich and back blows

steps.instruction: If coughing forcefully: encourage coughing. Do NOT intervene.
steps.helpDetail: A forceful cough means partial obstruction. The patient's own cough is more powerful than any technique you can apply. Stay ready but don't interfere. If coughing becomes weak, go to step 2.

steps.instruction: Stand behind, place fist above navel, thrust inward and upward sharply.
steps.helpDetail: Make a fist. Place thumb side against abdomen above the belly button, below the ribcage. Grasp fist with other hand. Quick upward thrust. Repeat until object is expelled or patient loses consciousness.

steps.instruction: If unconscious: lower to ground, begin CPR, check for visible object before each breath.
steps.helpDetail: Chest compressions on an unconscious choking victim may dislodge the object. Before each rescue breath, open the airway and look for the object. Only remove it if you can see it. Never blind finger sweep.

techniques.name: Junctional Hemorrhage Control
techniques.subtitle: Control bleeding at the neck, armpit, or groin where tourniquets can't go

steps.instruction: Pack gauze DEEP into the wound, pressing firmly against the bleeding vessel.
steps.helpDetail: Junctional areas (where limbs meet torso) cannot have tourniquets. Wound packing is the only option. Push gauze into the wound with your fingers, filling the entire cavity. Apply maximum pressure.

steps.instruction: Apply direct pressure for a minimum of 3 minutes without peeking.
steps.helpDetail: Pressure must be maintained continuously. Checking the wound releases the clot forming against the gauze. Time 3 full minutes. For major vessels, 10 minutes of pressure may be required.

steps.instruction: Apply pressure bandage over the packed wound. Do not remove initial packing.
steps.helpDetail: Wrap tightly around the packed wound. If blood soaks through, add more gauze on top — never remove the first layer. The initial gauze has clot formation on it. Removing it restarts the bleeding.

techniques.name: Poisoning Emergency Response
techniques.subtitle: Respond to ingested, inhaled, or absorbed poisons

steps.instruction: Identify the poison if possible. Preserve the container or a sample.
steps.helpDetail: Knowing what was ingested determines treatment. Check for containers, plants, mushrooms, chemicals. Note the time of exposure and estimated amount. Take photos of the substance if possible.

steps.instruction: Do NOT induce vomiting unless specifically instructed by poison control.
steps.helpDetail: Vomiting can cause additional harm with caustic substances (acids, alkalis) or petroleum products. Activated charcoal (if available) may be recommended for certain ingestions. Call poison control if possible.

steps.instruction: For inhaled poison: move to fresh air. For skin contact: remove clothing, flush with water.
steps.helpDetail: Inhaled toxins: move patient to fresh air immediately. Remove yourself if you feel symptoms. Skin exposure: remove contaminated clothing, flush skin with large amounts of water for 20+ minutes.

techniques.name: Frostbite Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Recognize and treat frozen tissue in extremities

steps.instruction: Identify frostbite stage: frostnip (red, painful) vs superficial (white/gray, numb) vs deep (hard, waxy, no feeling).
steps.helpDetail: Frostnip is reversible with warming. Superficial frostbite affects skin and subcutaneous tissue. Deep frostbite involves muscle, tendon, and bone — the tissue is frozen solid. Never rub frostbitten skin.

steps.instruction: Do NOT rewarm if there is any chance of refreezing. Refreezing causes catastrophic damage.
steps.helpDetail: Walking on frostbitten feet causes less damage than thawing and refreezing. If you cannot guarantee the tissue stays warm after rewarming, leave it frozen and evacuate. This is counterintuitive but medically critical.

steps.instruction: Rewarm in 98-102°F water (warm, not hot) for 20-30 minutes. Expect severe pain during rewarming.
steps.helpDetail: Immerse the affected area in warm water. Replenish water to maintain temperature. The patient will experience intense burning pain — this is normal and indicates blood returning. Give pain medication if available. Pad between fingers/toes with gauze after rewarming.

techniques.name: Snake Bite Response
techniques.subtitle: Manage venomous and non-venomous snake bites

steps.instruction: Move away from the snake. Note its appearance for identification. Do NOT attempt to catch it.
steps.helpDetail: Most second bites occur when people try to capture or kill the snake. Note: color pattern, head shape, size. Take a photo from safe distance if possible. Even a dead snake can deliver a reflex bite for up to an hour.

steps.instruction: Remove rings, watches, and tight clothing near the bite. Immobilize the bitten limb at or below heart level.
steps.helpDetail: Swelling can be rapid and severe. Constricting items will cut off circulation as tissue swells. Immobilize the limb like a fracture — movement increases venom spread. Keep the patient calm — elevated heart rate pumps venom faster.

steps.instruction: Do NOT: cut the wound, suck venom, apply tourniquet, or apply ice. Evacuate to medical facility.
steps.helpDetail: All traditional remedies (cutting, sucking, tourniquet) are ineffective and cause additional harm. The only treatment for envenomation is antivenom at a hospital. Mark the edge of swelling with a pen and the time — this helps doctors assess progression.

techniques.name: Spider & Scorpion Bite Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Identify and manage venomous arthropod envenomation

steps.instruction: Clean the bite area. Apply ice wrapped in cloth for 10 minutes on, 10 off.
steps.helpDetail: Most spider bites cause only local pain and swelling. Clean with soap and water. Cold reduces swelling and slows venom absorption. Elevate the bitten limb. Note the time of the bite.

steps.instruction: Watch for dangerous signs: severe pain spreading, muscle cramps, difficulty breathing, abdominal rigidity.
steps.helpDetail: Black widow: severe abdominal cramping, muscle rigidity, sweating, elevated blood pressure. Brown recluse: initially painless, then developing necrotic wound over 3-5 days. Scorpion: numbness, tingling, roving eye movements (especially in children).

steps.instruction: For severe reactions: keep patient calm, manage pain, and evacuate. Antivenom exists for black widow and bark scorpion.
steps.helpDetail: Deaths from spider bites are extremely rare in healthy adults. Children, elderly, and immunocompromised are highest risk. If breathing difficulty occurs, maintain airway and prepare for CPR. Take the spider with you to the hospital if safely possible.

techniques.name: Deep Splinter & Foreign Body Removal
techniques.subtitle: Safely remove embedded objects from skin

steps.instruction: Clean the area with soap and water. Sterilize tweezers or a needle with flame or alcohol.
steps.helpDetail: Wash hands thoroughly before starting. Sterilize tools by holding in flame until red, then let cool. Good lighting is essential — work in the best light available. Magnification helps for small splinters.

steps.instruction: For visible splinters: grasp with tweezers at the entry angle and pull straight out. For embedded: use a sterilized needle to expose the end.
steps.helpDetail: Pull in the direction the splinter entered — pulling at the wrong angle breaks it. For deeply embedded splinters: gently scrape the skin above with a sterilized needle until the end is exposed, then grasp with tweezers. Wooden splinters swell when wet — remove promptly.

steps.instruction: After removal: squeeze gently to promote bleeding (flushes bacteria), clean, apply antibiotic ointment.
steps.helpDetail: A small amount of bleeding after removal is desirable — it flushes contaminants. Clean with soap and water or antiseptic. Cover with a bandage. Watch for infection signs over 48 hours: increasing redness, warmth, swelling, pus, or red streaks.

techniques.name: Sprain Treatment (RICE Protocol)
techniques.subtitle: Manage ligament injuries with Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation

steps.instruction: REST: Stop using the injured joint immediately. Assess severity — can you bear weight?
steps.helpDetail: Grade 1: mild stretching, can bear weight with pain. Grade 2: partial tear, significant swelling, difficulty bearing weight. Grade 3: complete tear, unable to bear weight, severe instability. In survival, if you must move, splint and continue — but understand the risk of worsening.

steps.instruction: ICE: Apply cold for 20 minutes every 2 hours. Use stream water, snow, or cold wet cloth.
steps.helpDetail: Cold reduces swelling and pain. In the field: soak a cloth in cold stream water, pack snow in a shirt, or submerge the limb in cold water. Never apply ice directly to skin. Cold is most effective in the first 48 hours.

steps.instruction: COMPRESS: Wrap firmly with elastic bandage or cloth. ELEVATE: Keep above heart level when resting.
steps.helpDetail: Wrap from below the injury upward. Firm but not tight — check circulation every 30 minutes (fingers/toes should be warm and pink). Elevation uses gravity to reduce swelling. In a survival situation, take anti-inflammatory medication if available.

techniques.name: Knee Injury Stabilization
techniques.subtitle: Stabilize knee injuries for safe movement

steps.instruction: Assess the knee: can you straighten it? Does it lock? Is it grossly swollen or deformed?
steps.helpDetail: A locked knee (can't straighten) suggests a torn meniscus. Rapid swelling within 1-2 hours suggests ligament tear or fracture. Deformity suggests dislocation — this is an emergency. If the kneecap is displaced laterally, it may self-reduce when you straighten the leg.

steps.instruction: Splint the knee in a comfortable position — usually slightly bent (15-20 degrees).
steps.helpDetail: Use two padded rigid splints: one on each side of the leg from mid-thigh to mid-calf. A sleeping pad folded around the knee works well. Secure with straps or cloth ties above and below the knee. Leave toes visible to check circulation.

steps.instruction: If you must walk: create a rigid brace, use a walking stick on the opposite side, and take small steps.
steps.helpDetail: A trekking pole or sturdy branch on the opposite side takes weight off the injured knee. Wrap the knee tightly with cloth for compression. Walk downhill sideways to reduce stress on the knee. In survival: a non-functional knee makes evacuation the priority.

techniques.name: Femur Fracture Traction Splint
techniques.subtitle: Emergency stabilization of the most dangerous long bone fracture

steps.instruction: Recognize femur fracture: mid-thigh pain, deformity, shortened leg, rapid swelling. This is life-threatening.
steps.helpDetail: The femur is surrounded by the largest muscle mass in the body. When broken, muscles spasm and shorten the leg. Internal bleeding can exceed 1-2 liters into the thigh. Watch for shock. A femur fracture without traction causes continued bleeding and extreme pain.

steps.instruction: Apply traction: one rescuer stabilizes the hip, another applies steady pull at the ankle in line with the leg.
steps.helpDetail: Pull gently and steadily at the ankle — the goal is to restore leg length to normal. This opens the vascular space, reduces bleeding, and dramatically reduces pain. Maintain traction continuously. The pull force needed is roughly 10-15 pounds.

steps.instruction: Construct a traction splint from two long poles, padding, and a windlass to maintain pull.
steps.helpDetail: Improvised traction splint: two poles (one from armpit to foot, one from groin to foot), padded at contact points. Tie a loop around the ankle, secure to the end of the long pole. Use a windlass (stick twisted in a loop) to maintain traction. Secure the device with ties at hip, thigh, knee, and ankle. Check circulation in the foot every 15 minutes.

techniques.name: Trench Foot Prevention & Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Manage non-freezing cold injury from prolonged wet exposure

steps.instruction: Recognize trench foot: feet are cold, numb, pale or mottled, and may be swollen. Usually from 12+ hours in wet conditions.
steps.helpDetail: Trench foot occurs at temperatures above freezing (32-60°F) with prolonged wetness. It is NOT frostbite but is equally debilitating. The tissue damage comes from reduced blood flow, not freezing. Advanced cases: blistering, open sores, tissue death.

steps.instruction: Remove wet socks and shoes. Air dry feet. Warm gradually — do NOT use direct heat.
steps.helpDetail: Gently pat feet dry. Do not rub — the tissue is damaged. Elevate feet slightly. Warm gradually with body heat or ambient air. Direct heat (fire, hot water) causes additional damage to compromised tissue. The feet will become intensely painful as blood returns — this is expected.

steps.instruction: Prevention: change socks twice daily, dry feet at every opportunity, elevate when sleeping.
steps.helpDetail: Keep spare dry socks inside your clothing to warm them. Waterproof your footwear. At night, remove socks and let feet dry. Wiggle toes frequently to maintain circulation. In prolonged wet conditions, coat feet with animal fat or petroleum jelly as a moisture barrier.

techniques.name: Sprained Ankle Field Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Manage the most common wilderness injury

steps.instruction: Leave the boot ON if possible — it acts as a compression splint. Remove only to assess if swelling allows.
steps.helpDetail: Counterintuitive but critical: a snug boot provides compression and support. If you remove it, swelling may prevent you from putting it back on. Only remove to assess if you suspect fracture (point tenderness on bone) or if circulation is compromised.

steps.instruction: If boot removed: apply cold water, wrap with figure-8 bandage from toes to mid-calf.
steps.helpDetail: Submerge in a cold stream for 20 minutes. Wrap with an elastic bandage in a figure-8 pattern: start at the ball of the foot, cross over the ankle, around the heel, back over the ankle to the opposite side. Firm but not tight. Check toes for circulation.

steps.instruction: Fashion a walking aid: trekking pole or sturdy branch on the OPPOSITE side. Move slowly on flat terrain.
steps.helpDetail: The walking aid goes on the opposite side to the injury and steps forward with the injured leg. This takes significant weight off the ankle. Take small steps. Avoid uneven terrain. If the trail is steep, consider sitting and sliding downhill rather than walking.

techniques.name: Fever Management in the Field
techniques.subtitle: Reduce fever and prevent dangerous temperature elevation

steps.instruction: Monitor: fever below 102°F is the body fighting infection. Above 104°F is dangerous. Cool actively.
steps.helpDetail: Fever is a beneficial immune response — suppressing a mild fever may prolong illness. Focus on hydration, not temperature reduction, for fevers under 102°F. Above 104°F: active cooling is required. Above 106°F: brain damage risk — emergency.

steps.instruction: Cool the patient: remove excess clothing, apply wet cloths to forehead, neck, armpits, and groin.
steps.helpDetail: These areas have major blood vessels near the skin surface. Wet cloths with cool (not cold) water. Fan the patient if possible. Do NOT cause shivering — shivering generates heat and is counterproductive. If shivering starts, reduce cooling.

steps.instruction: Push fluids aggressively. Give ibuprofen or acetaminophen if available.
steps.helpDetail: Fever increases fluid loss by 10% for every degree above normal. Oral rehydration is critical. Alternate ibuprofen and acetaminophen every 3 hours for maximum effect. Willow bark tea is a natural salicylate (aspirin-like) if no medication is available. Watch for dehydration signs: dark urine, dry mouth, headache.

articles.title: Controlling Bleeding
articles.body:
Blood loss kills faster than almost any other trauma. A person can bleed to death from a severed femoral artery in 3-5 minutes.

Bleeding control priority:
1. Direct pressure — your hands and a cloth on the wound
2. Pressure bandage — wrap tightly (Israeli/emergency bandage)
3. Tourniquet — for life-threatening limb bleeding only
4. Wound packing — for junctional areas (groin, armpit, neck)

Tourniquet myths debunked: Modern evidence shows tourniquets can stay on for 6+ hours without limb loss. The old 'you'll lose the limb' teaching is wrong. A loose tourniquet is more dangerous than a tight one — it blocks venous return while allowing arterial flow, increasing bleeding.

Wound packing: For deep wounds that won't stop bleeding, pack gauze INTO the wound, pressing firmly against the bleeding vessel. This feels wrong but saves lives.

articles.title: CPR: What You Need to Know
articles.body:
Compression-only CPR (hands-only) is nearly as effective as full CPR for adults who collapse suddenly. If you're unwilling or unable to give mouth-to-mouth, compressions alone still save lives.

Key numbers:
- 100-120 compressions per minute (beat of 'Stayin' Alive')
- 2 inches deep minimum for adults
- Full chest recoil between compressions
- 30 compressions : 2 breaths ratio

Quality degrades rapidly. Switch rescuers every 2 minutes. Most people push too shallow after 1 minute of fatigue.

For children: same ratio but use one hand. For infants: two fingers, 1.5 inches deep.

AED (Automated External Defibrillator): If available, use immediately. They are designed for untrained users. Open the case and follow voice prompts. You cannot hurt someone with an AED — it only shocks if needed.

articles.title: Fracture Recognition & Management
articles.body:
Assume a fracture exists if there is:
- Deformity (limb looks wrong)
- Swelling at a specific point
- Crepitus (grinding sensation)
- Loss of function
- Point tenderness (pain at one specific spot when pressed)

Open fracture (bone visible through skin): This is a surgical emergency. Cover the wound with a moist sterile dressing. Do NOT push bone back in. Splint and evacuate.

Femur fractures are life-threatening — the thigh can hold 1-2 liters of internal blood. Watch for shock.

General splinting rules:
- Splint in position found
- Immobilize joint above AND below the break
- Pad all bony prominences
- Check circulation every 15 minutes
- Never straighten a deformity in the field

articles.title: Burn Classification & Treatment
articles.body:
Burns are classified by depth:

1st degree (superficial): Red, painful, no blisters. Like a sunburn. Treat with cool water and aloe vera. Heals in 3-5 days.

2nd degree (partial thickness): Red, painful, blisters present. Very painful because nerve endings are intact but exposed. Cool water 20 min, do not pop blisters, clean bandage. Heals in 2-3 weeks.

3rd degree (full thickness): White/brown/black, often painless (nerves are destroyed). Requires skin grafting. Do not cool — wrap and evacuate.

The Rule of Nines for estimating burn area:
- Head: 9%
- Each arm: 9%
- Chest front: 18%
- Back: 18%
- Each leg: 18%
- Groin: 1%

Burns over 20% body surface area require IV fluid resuscitation. This is a hospital emergency.

articles.title: Venomous Bites & Stings
articles.body:
Snake bites: Only 20% of bites from venomous snakes actually envenomate (dry bites are common). But ALWAYS treat as envenomated until proven otherwise. The only treatment is antivenom at a hospital. Everything else (cutting, sucking, tourniquet, electric shock) makes it worse.

Spider bites: Brown recluse causes tissue necrosis — clean, ice, and evacuate. Black widow causes muscle cramps and pain — seek medical care but rarely fatal in adults.

Scorpion stings: Most are painful but not dangerous. Exception: Bark scorpion (southwestern US) can cause neurological symptoms in children. Seek medical care.

Tick-borne diseases: Lyme disease (bull's-eye rash), Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever (spotted rash on palms/soles), and many others. Early antibiotic treatment is critical.

articles.title: Environmental Emergencies
articles.body:
Hypothermia stages:
- Mild (95-89°F): Shivering, confusion, poor coordination
- Moderate (89-82°F): Shivering stops, severe confusion, drowsiness
- Severe (<82°F): Unconscious, very slow pulse, appears dead

The phrase 'nobody is dead until they're warm and dead' exists because severely hypothermic patients have survived with no detectable pulse. Continue CPR and rewarm.

Heat illness spectrum:
Heat cramps → Heat exhaustion → Heat stroke

The critical difference: heat exhaustion patients are sweating. Heat stroke patients have stopped sweating. Heat stroke = emergency department NOW.

Drowning: Start CPR immediately. 5 rescue breaths first (water in lungs prevents oxygen exchange). Do not attempt to drain water. Begin standard CPR protocol. People have survived 30+ minutes of submersion in cold water.

Lightning: Lightning victims do NOT carry a charge. They are safe to touch. Start CPR immediately. Lightning strikes have high survival rates with prompt CPR.

articles.title: Anaphylaxis & Allergic Reactions
articles.body:
Anaphylaxis is a whole-body allergic reaction that can kill in minutes. Common triggers: bee stings, peanuts, shellfish, medications, latex.

Symptom progression:
1. Skin: hives, flushing, itching
2. Swelling: face, lips, tongue, throat
3. Respiratory: wheezing, stridor, shortness of breath
4. Cardiovascular: rapid pulse, low blood pressure, dizziness
5. GI: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea

Epinephrine is the ONLY first-line treatment. Antihistamines (Benadryl) are too slow to save a life. EpiPen goes in the outer thigh, through clothing is fine.

Biphasic reaction: Up to 20% of anaphylaxis patients have a second wave 1-72 hours later. Hospital observation for at least 4 hours is essential.

articles.title: Survival First Aid Kit Essentials
articles.body:
Minimum wilderness first aid kit:

Bleeding control:
- Tourniquet (CAT or SOFTT-W)
- Israeli bandage
- Hemostatic gauze (QuikClot)
- Roller gauze (2x)
- Medical tape

Wound care:
- Irrigation syringe
- Wound closure strips (Steri-Strips)
- Tincture of benzoin
- Antibiotic ointment
- Non-stick pads

Medications:
- Ibuprofen 200mg
- Diphenhydramine 25mg (Benadryl)
- Loperamide (Imodium)
- Electrolyte packets
- Epinephrine auto-injector (if prescribed)

Tools:
- Trauma shears
- Fine-point tweezers
- Safety pins
- Nitrile gloves (4+ pairs)
- SAM splint
- Moleskin
- Elastic bandage (ACE wrap)
- Emergency blanket

All items should be checked and refreshed annually. Medications expire. Adhesives degrade.

articles.title: Triage & Treatment Priorities
articles.body:
When someone is injured, assess in this order (MARCH):

M — Massive hemorrhage: Stop life-threatening bleeding FIRST. This kills fastest.

A — Airway: Is the airway open? Head tilt, chin lift. Clear obstructions.

R — Respiration: Are they breathing? Look, listen, feel. Start rescue breathing if not.

C — Circulation: Is there a pulse? Start CPR if not. Treat for shock.

H — Hypothermia/Head injury: Prevent heat loss. Assess level of consciousness.

The biggest mistake in field medicine is treating the dramatic wound while missing the critical one. A tourniquet on a spurting artery takes priority over a broken arm.

Scene safety: ALWAYS ensure the scene is safe before providing care. You cannot help if you become a victim. Check for traffic, fire, electrical hazards, aggressive animals, or ongoing violence.

articles.title: Wound Infection Prevention
articles.body:
In survival, infection is the slow killer. Every wound becomes a race against bacteria.

Signs of infection (usually appear 24-72 hours after injury):
- Increasing redness spreading from wound edges
- Warmth around the wound
- Swelling that worsens over time
- Pus (white, yellow, or green drainage)
- Red streaks radiating from the wound (lymphangitis — URGENT)
- Fever and chills (systemic infection — EMERGENCY)

Prevention steps:
1. Clean the wound immediately with clean water (irrigation pressure matters more than sterility)
2. Remove all debris and foreign material
3. Apply antibiotic ointment if available
4. Change dressings every 12-24 hours
5. Keep the wound dry between changes

Field antiseptics when nothing else is available:
- Diluted iodine (2-3 drops per liter of clean water)
- Honey (raw honey has natural antibacterial properties)
- Salt water (1 teaspoon per cup)
- Clean urine is NOT antiseptic — this is a myth

Critical rule: a wound that is getting worse after 48 hours needs antibiotics or surgical care. Field treatment can only buy time.

articles.title: Pediatric Emergencies
articles.body:
Children are NOT small adults. Their physiology differs in critical ways that affect emergency treatment.

Key differences:
- Higher heart rate (infant: 120-160 bpm is NORMAL)
- Higher respiratory rate (infant: 30-60 breaths/min is NORMAL)
- Proportionally larger head (higher spinal injury risk)
- Less blood volume (small blood loss = big problem)
- Faster dehydration (higher surface area to volume ratio)
- Better compensation until sudden collapse (children 'look fine' until they crash)

CPR modifications:
- Infant (under 1 year): 2 fingers, 1.5 inches deep, 30:2 ratio
- Child (1-8 years): 1 hand, 2 inches deep, 30:2 ratio
- Ventilation: gentle puffs only — too much pressure damages small lungs

Choking:
- Infant: 5 back blows + 5 chest thrusts (NO abdominal thrusts)
- Child over 1: standard Heimlich maneuver

Psychological first aid:
- Children respond to calm, reassuring voices
- Let them hold a parent or familiar object
- Explain what you're doing in simple terms
- Never lie about pain — say 'this will hurt but it will help you'
- Their fear can escalate an emergency if not managed

articles.title: Improvised Medical Tools
articles.body:
When proper medical supplies aren't available, improvisation saves lives.

Improvised tourniquet:
- Material: belt, strip of cloth, bandana (at least 1.5 inches wide)
- Windlass: stick, pen, spoon handle
- Apply 2-3 inches above wound, tighten until bleeding stops
- Mark time of application on patient's forehead

Improvised splint materials:
- SAM splint alternatives: folded cardboard, newspaper, sticks, trekking poles
- Padding: clothing, moss, leaves
- Ties: torn fabric strips, shoelaces, tape, paracord

Improvised wound closure:
- Butterfly strips from tape (cut into hourglass shape)
- Super glue on SUPERFICIAL cuts only (not deep wounds)
- Hair: tie strands across the wound to hold edges together
- Thorns as suture pins in extreme situations

Improvised irrigation syringe:
- Plastic bag with a small hole poked in the corner
- Water bottle with hole in cap
- Goal: pressurized stream to flush debris from wounds

Improvised cervical collar:
- Rolled towel or jacket wrapped around neck
- Secured with tape, strips, or safety pins
- Must NOT compress the airway

articles.title: Understanding Crush Syndrome
articles.body:
Crush syndrome is one of the most counterintuitive killers in disaster medicine. The patient seems fine while trapped — then dies within minutes of being freed.

What happens:
- Prolonged pressure on muscles (1+ hours) causes cell death
- Dead muscle cells accumulate potassium, myoglobin, and lactate
- When pressure is released, these toxins flood the bloodstream
- Potassium stops the heart. Myoglobin destroys the kidneys.

Timeline of danger:
- Crush time < 1 hour: usually safe to release immediately
- Crush time 1-4 hours: moderate risk. Tourniquet before release if possible
- Crush time 4+ hours: high risk. Do NOT release without medical preparation

Field management:
1. If possible, start IV fluids BEFORE releasing the crushing object (1L/hr normal saline)
2. Apply tourniquet above crush site before release
3. After release: push fluids aggressively (oral if IV unavailable)
4. Monitor heart rhythm — sudden cardiac arrest is the primary killer
5. Transport to hospital immediately — dialysis may be needed

In mass casualty events (earthquakes, building collapse), crush syndrome is a leading cause of delayed death. Knowledge of this condition saves lives that are otherwise lost to well-intentioned but uninformed rescue.

articles.title: Environmental Emergencies: Heat & Cold
articles.body:
The human body operates in a narrow temperature range. Deviation in either direction can be fatal.

Heat Spectrum:
- Heat cramps: muscle spasms from salt loss. Treatment: rest, water, salt.
- Heat exhaustion: heavy sweating, weakness, nausea. Temp < 104°F. Treatment: cool environment, fluids.
- Heat stroke: altered mental status, temp > 104°F. EMERGENCY. Active cooling required immediately.

Key distinction: heat exhaustion = sweating and aware. Heat stroke = confused/combative. If in doubt, treat as heat stroke.

Cold Spectrum:
- Mild hypothermia (90-95°F): shivering, poor coordination. Treatment: warm environment, dry clothes, warm drinks.
- Moderate hypothermia (82-90°F): shivering stops, confusion, slurred speech. Treatment: active rewarming of core.
- Severe hypothermia (<82°F): unconscious, barely detectable pulse. Handle GENTLY. Active core rewarming.

Critical cold rules:
- 'Nobody is dead until they're warm and dead' — always attempt resuscitation
- Do NOT warm extremities before core (causes afterdrop)
- Do NOT give alcohol (dilates blood vessels, accelerates heat loss)
- Do NOT rub frostbitten skin (causes tissue damage)

articles.title: Airway Management Priorities
articles.body:
Without an open airway, nothing else matters. A patient can survive massive bleeding for minutes, but complete airway obstruction kills in 4-6 minutes.

Common causes of airway obstruction:
- Tongue falling back (unconscious patient lying face-up)
- Blood, vomit, or broken teeth
- Swelling from burns, allergic reaction, or trauma
- Foreign body (food, objects)

Basic airway interventions (no equipment needed):
1. Head-tilt chin-lift: tilt forehead back, lift chin. Opens airway in most unconscious patients.
2. Jaw thrust: if spinal injury suspected. Push jaw forward without moving neck.
3. Recovery position: unconscious but breathing — roll on side. Gravity clears the airway.
4. Finger sweep: ONLY if you can see the obstruction. Never blind sweep.

Advanced considerations:
- Snoring = partial obstruction (tongue). Reposition head.
- Gurgling = fluid in airway. Turn patient on side, suction if available.
- Stridor (high-pitched on inhale) = upper airway swelling. This is an emergency.
- Silence with chest movement = complete obstruction. Immediate intervention needed.

The most important airway skill: recognizing that the airway is compromised. Check airway FIRST in every patient assessment.

articles.title: Systematic Trauma Assessment
articles.body:
In a trauma situation, systematic assessment prevents you from missing life-threatening injuries.

Primary Survey (ABCDE) — find and fix immediate killers:
- A: Airway — is it open? Clear obstructions. Protect cervical spine.
- B: Breathing — is the chest rising equally? Listen for breath sounds. Seal chest wounds.
- C: Circulation — control major bleeding. Check pulse. Note skin color.
- D: Disability — consciousness level (alert, voice, pain, unresponsive). Pupil response.
- E: Exposure — remove clothing to find hidden injuries. Prevent hypothermia.

Secondary Survey — detailed head-to-toe exam (only after ABCDE is stable):
1. Head: skull deformity, blood from ears/nose, pupil asymmetry
2. Neck: tenderness, deformity, trachea midline?
3. Chest: symmetrical? Tender ribs? Crepitus (grinding)?
4. Abdomen: rigid? Tender? Distended?
5. Pelvis: stable when gently pressed? (Only test ONCE)
6. Extremities: deformity, swelling, circulation/sensation/motion check
7. Back: log-roll to check. Tenderness along spine?

Reassess every 5 minutes. Conditions change. What was stable becomes unstable. Document findings and times.

articles.title: Essential Medications for Wilderness First Aid
articles.body:
A basic medication kit can address most common wilderness medical problems.

Pain management:
- Ibuprofen (Advil): anti-inflammatory + pain relief. 400-800mg every 6-8 hours. Best for musculoskeletal pain, headaches.
- Acetaminophen (Tylenol): pain + fever reduction. 500-1000mg every 6 hours. Safe with most other medications.
- Combination: ibuprofen + acetaminophen together is more effective than either alone and safe to combine.

Gastrointestinal:
- Loperamide (Imodium): stops diarrhea. Critical in wilderness to prevent dehydration. 4mg initially, then 2mg after each loose stool.
- Bismuth subsalicylate (Pepto-Bismol): nausea, heartburn, mild diarrhea.
- Oral rehydration salts: for rehydrating after fluid loss.

Allergy/Anaphylaxis:
- Diphenhydramine (Benadryl): antihistamine for allergic reactions. 25-50mg every 6 hours. Causes drowsiness.
- Epinephrine auto-injector: for anaphylaxis ONLY. Prescription required. Carry two.

Wound care:
- Antibiotic ointment (bacitracin/Neosporin): for minor wounds to prevent infection
- Povidone-iodine: wound cleaning and water purification

All medications have expiration dates but most retain 90%+ potency for years past expiration. In a survival situation, expired medication is better than no medication.
